findings.title: K-SEC-1 Unix-socket guard bypass: libpq multi-host comma value (e.g. host=/tmp,evil.com) is accepted, allowing off-box exfiltration
findings.location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_local.py:41-53 (is_local_socket_path); exploited at lines 152-153 (URI query branch) and 164-165 (DSN branch) of require_local_uri
findings.claim: is_local_socket_path() only inspects the leading characters of host (h.startswith('/') and not '//'/'\\'); it never rejects a comma. libpq treats a comma in the host value as a MULTI-HOST separator, so host='/tmp,evil.com' means [Unix socket /tmp, TCP host evil.com] tried in order. Because is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com') returns True, both the URI-query branch (require_local_uri lines 152-153) and the libpq DSN branch (lines 164-165) ACCEPT such a value. Verified by execution: require_local_uri("host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x", what='t') -> ACCEPT; require_local_uri('postgresql:///db?host=/tmp,evil.com', what='t') -> ACCEPT; require_local_uri('postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp,evil.com', what='t') -> ACCEPT. is_local_socket_path('/var/run/postgresql,attacker.com:5432') -> True.
findings.why_real: checkpoint.py:163 (get_postgres_connection_string) and observability/tracking.py:30 route operator/env-supplied connection strings (ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL, MLflow tracking URI) through require_local_uri precisely to enforce ADR-0003 (graph/checkpoint state must never leave the box). A connection string like 'host=/var/run/postgresql,attacker.com dbname=assay' passes the guard, but libpq will fall through to TCP attacker.com whenever the local socket is unavailable (socket missing, wrong dir, permission denied) — silently shipping checkpoint/experiment data off-box, the exact failure ADR-0003 forbids. The authority branch already defends against multi-host via _authority_hosts() comma-splitting (audit #D3), but the socket-path acceptance added for K-SEC-1 was only wired into the query-param and DSN branches, which do NOT split on commas — so K-SEC-1 reopened the multi-host hole specifically for socket-shaped values.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the socket gate reject the libpq multi-host separator. In is_local_socket_path (_local.py:51-53), add `if ',' in h: return False` before the leading-slash check (a genuine local socket directory has no commas). Equivalently/additionally, split each host/hostaddr value on ',' in both the query-param branch (lines 150-157) and the DSN branch (lines 161-169) and require every element to satisfy is_loopback_host or is_local_socket_path, mirroring the comma-splitting already done by _authority_hosts(). Add regression tests asserting REJECT for 'host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x', 'postgresql:///db?host=/tmp,evil.com', and 'postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp,evil.com'.

findings.title: Operator's internal hostname ([REDACTED] / hc-grc.local) leaks into committed transcripts; no scrub pattern covers it
findings.location: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/scripts/capture_transcripts.py:38-216 (_SECRET_PATTERNS + _scrub: no hostname rule); leaked in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/transcripts/sessions/8d54714a-d616-4911-a386-9b4b0f2ce74d/subagents/workflows/wf_82001839-351/agent-a61cd08ca3c6d93a2.jsonl and wf_496c86b1-0fc/agent-a6c39cf0b12e80f58.jsonl and wf_574c42b7-0aa/agent-ad2e7e5365e7451dd.jsonl
findings.claim: The capture scrubber redacts email, home-dir username, display name, and GitHub handle, but has NO rule for the machine hostname. The operator's internal hostname appears in the committed corpus as the strings '[REDACTED]' and 'hc-grc.local' (14 raw occurrences across 2+ files). At least one is genuine data, not meta-discussion: a `user`-role message embeds the PROV-O RDF namespace `hcgrc: <https://hc-grc.local/prov#>` read from a real hc-grc project file. `hostname`/`scutil --get LocalHostName`/`--get ComputerName` on this node all return [REDACTED], and per the operator memory this Mac mini is the named production node, so the hostname is operator/infrastructure-identifying PII.
findings.why_real: Verified live: `scutil --get LocalHostName` and `ComputerName` both return `[REDACTED]`; `hostname` returns `[REDACTED]`. Importing the scrubber and calling capture_transcripts._scrub('ran on [REDACTED] node') returns the string UNCHANGED — confirming no pattern matches the hostname. The string is present in committed files (grep -rl finds it), so it ships in the repo and git history. This is a distinct identity class from everything pass 7 covered (handle/name/username/email/ReDoS).
findings.suggested_fix: Add a hostname-derivation rule analogous to _username_pattern/_repo_handle_patterns: derive candidates from socket.gethostname(), platform.node(), and (on macOS) `scutil --get LocalHostName`/`ComputerName`, plus an ASSAY_SCRUB_HOSTS env; for each (length-guarded, >=4 chars) redact the bare name and the `<name>.local` mDNS form case-insensitively. Run it inside _scrub after the existing rules. Add a regression test asserting [REDACTED] is redacted, and document the hostname class in transcripts/README.md scope/caveats.

findings.title: transcripts/README.md scope caveat understates redaction (email) and omits the new identity classes actually scrubbed/leaking
findings.claim: The README says only 'Credential-shaped secrets ... are redacted' and that transcripts 'may contain the operator's email and internal reasoning.' In reality the scrubber now redacts email/username/display-name/GitHub-handle (so the email claim is stale — emails ARE redacted to [REDACTED]@protonmail.com), while the hostname ([REDACTED]) is NOT redacted and is NOT mentioned. A reader relying on this caveat to decide whether to publish gets an inaccurate picture of both what is removed and what residual identity vector remains.
findings.why_real: Grep confirms emails in the corpus appear only as `[REDACTED]@protonmail.com` (local part scrubbed), contradicting 'may contain the operator's email'; meanwhile `[REDACTED]` appears raw and is unmentioned. The doc is the operator's stated gate for public release (line 24 'read before publishing'), so its inaccuracy is a real pre-publication-decision hazard, not a style nit.
findings.suggested_fix: Update the caveat to: (a) state emails, home-dir username, operator display name, and GitHub handle are redacted; (b) list the residual classes that are NOT scrubbed (filesystem path structure, internal reasoning, and — until P9-PII-1 is fixed — internal hostnames); keep the email line accurate (domain remnant only).

findings.title: K-REL-1 SIGTERM re-delivery still swallows termination when prior handler is C-installed (getsignal returns None)
findings.claim: The K-REL-1 fix handles only two prior-disposition cases: callable (chain) and signal.SIG_DFL (reset+re-deliver). But signal.getsignal() is documented to return None when 'an unknown handler is in effect' i.e. SIGTERM was installed by C code / a native extension (common under gunicorn workers, embedded interpreters, some async runtimes, or a native signalfd shim). For prior=None: callable(None) is False, None == signal.SIG_DFL is False, None == signal.SIG_IGN is False — so NEITHER branch fires. The handler flushes, shuts down, and simply returns, having replaced SIGTERM's action with flush-and-return. The process then ignores SIGTERM exactly as in the original K-REL-1 bug (unkillable by `docker stop`/`kill`/k8s until SIGKILL escalation), just for the None-prior case instead of the SIG_DFL case the fix targeted.
findings.why_real: Verified against python: signal.getsignal docstring explicitly lists 'None -- if an unknown handler is in effect'. Reproduced the decision logic: for prior=None all three branches are False, so the handler returns without re-establishing default termination or re-delivering. The original handler is not re-entered, so no recovery occurs. This is the same failure mode K-REL-1 claimed to close, on a real and not-exotic prior disposition.
findings.suggested_fix: Treat the 'cannot chain, cannot confirm intentional ignore' case as terminate-by-default: change the elif to also cover None, e.g. `elif prior == signal.SIG_DFL or prior is None:` so a C-installed (unknown) prior handler falls through to reset SIG_DFL + os.kill re-delivery. Only an explicit `prior == signal.SIG_IGN` should be allowed to swallow SIGTERM. Add a regression test that monkeypatches tr.signal.getsignal to return None and asserts SIG_DFL is re-established and os.kill is called.

findings.title: K-OBS-2 default=str on the tracker artifact diverges from persist_trail(), which still raises on the same hashable-but-non-JSON payload
findings.location: src/assay_engine/pipeline.py:756-758 (_log_trail json.dumps default=str) vs. pipeline.py:240-243 (StudyResult.persist_trail json.dumps with no default)
findings.claim: K-OBS-2 added default=str to the tracker-artifact serialization so a payload carrying a hashable-but-non-JSON leaf (bytes/datetime/Enum — all accepted by ProvenanceTrail.record) no longer orphans a temp file or silently drops the #G-009 artifact. But StudyResult.persist_trail() — the documented durable, re-verifiable audit trail that 'round-trips through from_records / verify_records' — still calls json.dumps WITHOUT default=str. So the very same trail that _log_trail writes successfully (artifact attaches green, values silently stringified) makes persist_trail raise TypeError. The two serialization paths for one trail now disagree: the tracker artifact and the on-disk audit trail are not the same bytes, and the path operators are told to use for durable audit is the one that breaks. Additionally, default=str coerces a bytes leaf to its repr (e.g. b'...' -> "b'...'"), so any sensitive bytes that previously errored-out (and were never written) now land in the tracker artifact in stringified form.
findings.why_real: Reproduced directly: ProvenanceTrail().record('x','msg', when=datetime(...), blob=b'secret-bytes') is accepted; entries_to_records then json.dumps (persist_trail path) raises 'Object of type datetime is not JSON serializable', while json.dumps(..., default=str) (the _log_trail path) succeeds producing payload {"when":"2020-01-01 00:00...", blob stringified}. The fix hardened one of two serialization sites for the identical input, leaving an inconsistency and a broken primary persistence path.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the two paths consistent: either (a) give persist_trail the same default=str fallback so the durable audit trail and tracker artifact agree, or (b) validate payload leaves are JSON-native at record() time so neither path can receive bytes/datetime/Enum and remove default=str so a genuinely unserializable payload still fails loud. Prefer (b) for an audit artifact, since default=str silently makes the trail non-round-trippable (and can leak stringified secret bytes); if keeping (a), document that the artifact is lossy and de-dup the json.dumps call into one helper used by both sites.

findings.title: confirm primitives raise opaque TypeError (not the documented ValueError) when alpha is None on both hypothesis and call
findings.claim: Hypothesis.alpha defaults to None and is explicitly valid as None at construction (hypothesis.py:79,93 — 'Left None for exploratory/opted-out use'). When a study locks such a hypothesis and calls confirm_unit_level(..., alpha=None) or confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha=None), _resolve_locked_param(None, None, 'alpha') returns None, then _validate_alpha(None) executes `0.0 < None` and raises `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'float' and 'NoneType'` instead of the contract error `ValueError: alpha must be in (0, 0.5)`. Reproduced live for both primitives.
findings.why_real: alpha=None is a first-class, documented hypothesis state (deferred-to-confirm-time, mirroring predicted_direction). The confirm primitives are part of the public methodology API a study drives directly (confirm.py docstrings explicitly describe direct use outside a runner). A direct caller who legitimately deferred alpha and forgot to supply it at confirm time gets an internal numeric-comparison TypeError that names neither parameter nor the valid range — an opaque failure in the methodology layer whose whole purpose is to validate confirmatory inputs loudly (issue #4). It is not silently wrong, but it violates the module's own typed-error contract.
findings.suggested_fix: In _resolve_locked_param or at the top of each confirm primitive, after resolving alpha, raise a clear ValueError when the resolved alpha is None: e.g. `if alpha is None: raise ValueError('alpha must be supplied at confirm time or pre-registered on the hypothesis; got None')` before _validate_alpha. Same guard for the resolved stability_threshold path (it is defaulted to 0.9 so it is safe, but alpha has no default).

findings.title: Bare int()/float() env parsing in seam.py and checkpoint.py crashes import with opaque error — the exact class fixed (with _int_env) in tracing.py and vectorstore.py
findings.claim: tracing.py:32-43 and vectorstore.py:22-26 both wrap int() in an `_int_env` helper that raises `ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}")` — explicitly to avoid the 'opaque invalid literal for int()' import-time crash (commented #CV-S-1, #H-022). But seam.py and checkpoint.py still use bare int()/float() over os.environ at module scope. Verified live: `ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT=abc python -c 'import assay_engine.reasoning.seam'` dies with `ValueError: could not convert string to float: 'abc'` at seam.py:94, and `ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=xyz python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint'` dies with `invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'xyz'` at checkpoint.py:61 — both at import time, before any handler, naming neither the env var nor the expected format.
findings.why_real: Falsifiable env-var-parsing defect with a concrete failure mode: a single mistyped ASSAY_* env var crashes module import of the reasoning seam (the documented LLM tier) or the persistence/checkpoint backend with an error that does not identify which knob is wrong. It is a genuine fix-regression — the maintainers identified and fixed exactly this class in two modules (with a named-error helper) but left two other modules, including the persistence backend, unpatched. Low severity because it fails loud (no silent misbehavior) and only fires on operator misconfiguration.
findings.suggested_fix: Hoist the existing `_int_env` pattern (and an analogous `_float_env`) into a shared helper (e.g. in _local or a small env util) and use it for every numeric ASSAY_* parse in seam.py (BULK_TIMEOUT, BULK_NUM_PREDICT, MAX_RETRIES, BACKOFF_BASE, RATE_LIMIT_*, JSON_REROLLS, RUN_JSON_DEADLINE) and checkpoint.py (_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS and the ASSAY_POOL_* parses at 271-273), so a bad value raises a message that names the var and the expected type.

findings.title: BULK httpx timeout leaves write+read phases each at full BULK_TIMEOUT, so worst-case total exceeds the outer _with_timeout bound and leaks a pool worker slot
findings.location: src/assay_engine/reasoning/seam.py:356-369 (_bulk_complete, the httpx.Timeout construction); contradicting invariant asserted at lines 352-355 and partially tested at tests/test_reasoning.py:363-381
findings.claim: _bulk_complete builds `httpx.Timeout(BULK_TIMEOUT, connect=min(10,BULK_TIMEOUT), pool=min(10,BULK_TIMEOUT))`. The positional first argument is the default only for categories not explicitly named, so it sets BOTH read=BULK_TIMEOUT AND write=BULK_TIMEOUT; only connect and pool are reduced. httpx applies each phase timeout independently. The inline comment (lines 353-355) claims this keeps 'the TOTAL ~BULK_TIMEOUT (+ a small connect)' and exists specifically to prevent a slot leak past the outer bound. But the real worst case for a hung backend is connect (10s) + write (up to BULK_TIMEOUT) + read (up to BULK_TIMEOUT) ~= 10 + 2*BULK_TIMEOUT (~250s at defaults), which exceeds the outer `_with_timeout(..., BULK_TIMEOUT + 10, ...)` bound (~130s) used at seam.py:537. When the outer FuturesTimeout fires, the worker is NOT cancelled (seam.py:264-267) and keeps its slot until the still-running backend call finally returns at the inner phase timeout — i.e. up to ~2x the headroom past the bound. The test at test_reasoning.py:378-381 only asserts `connect + read <= BULK_TIMEOUT + 10`; it never checks `timeout.write`, so the write phase (also = BULK_TIMEOUT) is unbounded relative to the outer headroom and the gap is invisible to the suite.
findings.why_real: Verified by reading the code and the test. httpx documents that the positional Timeout argument is the default for unspecified categories (read and write here), and that connect/read/write/pool are independent per-operation budgets; the code only overrides connect and pool. The outer wall-clock bound is BULK_TIMEOUT+10 (seam.py:537) while the inner phases sum to ~2*BULK_TIMEOUT+connect for a backend that stalls in both the write (slow request-body consumption / slow-loris read) and read phases. The slot-leak failure mode is exactly the one the saturation guard (seam.py:223-231) depends on NOT happening: a leaked slot held ~2x past the bound erodes the 8-slot pool and surfaces as spurious RateLimitError backpressure to unrelated callers. The likelihood is low (LLM request bodies are small, so the write phase rarely stalls), hence low severity, but it is a genuine, falsifiable contradiction of the module's own stated invariant.
findings.suggested_fix: Set the write (and read) phase explicitly so no single phase plus connect can exceed the outer headroom. E.g. construct `httpx.Timeout(connect=min(10.0, BULK_TIMEOUT), read=BULK_TIMEOUT, write=min(10.0, BULK_TIMEOUT), pool=min(10.0, BULK_TIMEOUT))` (write is request-send and need not equal the long read budget), OR raise the outer `_with_timeout` headroom in seam.py:537 to cover the true worst-case sum of independent phases. Extend the test at tests/test_reasoning.py:378-381 to also assert `timeout.write` is bounded and that `connect + read + write <= BULK_TIMEOUT + 10` so the invariant the comment claims is actually enforced.

findings.title: No enforced required status check: red CI does not block merges (the exact mechanism behind "silently red for 3 passes")
findings.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml (all-checks job, lines ~begin of `all-checks:`) + repo branch-protection config
findings.claim: The CI design comment states "branch protection should require ONLY all-checks" and the all-checks fan-in job exists to be that single gate. But branch protection is NOT enabled/available on this repo (gh api repos/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/branches/main/protection returns HTTP 403 "Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public to enable this feature"). Therefore all-checks (and core/integration/audit) gate nothing at merge time. `gh run list --branch main` shows merge commits for PRs #161, #162, #166, #167, #168 all with CI conclusion=failure — i.e. they were merged to main and CI then ran red on the merge commit. CI runs `on: push: branches:[main]` and `pull_request`, so a failing PR run never prevented the merge.
findings.why_real: Falsifiable from two independent signals: (1) the branch-protection API explicitly reports the feature is unavailable on this plan, so no required check can be configured; (2) the run history lists 5 main-branch merge commits with conclusion=failure, proving merges of red branches actually occurred. This is the precise failure mode the pass-9 dimension was created to catch: the all-checks gate is purely decorative without an enforced required status check, so CI can be (and was) silently red across passes while merges proceeded.
findings.suggested_fix: Either make the repo public / upgrade the plan and configure branch protection on main to require the `all-checks` status check (and disallow merging PRs whose checks are not green), or — since branch protection is unavailable — change the workflow trigger strategy so the gate is enforceable: gate on PR check status before merge via the merge tooling (e.g. `gh pr merge --auto` only after checks pass), and add a self-check that fails the run if it detects it ran on a push to main with a non-success result that was not pre-gated. At minimum, document in the hardening protocol that merges must be blocked manually until `all-checks` is green, since nothing in the platform enforces it.

findings.title: Coverage floor enforced only in the all-extras integration lane; the no-extras core lane has no coverage gate
findings.location: .github/workflows/ci.yml — core job `Test (pytest)` step (`pytest -q`) vs integration job `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-fail-under=90`
findings.claim: Coverage with --cov-fail-under=90 runs ONLY in the integration lane (all extras + service containers). The core lane runs bare `pytest -q` with no coverage measurement. Coverage of the package is therefore reported under the full-backend configuration; the pure-Python fallback branches that execute in the no-extras core lane (e.g. baseline primitives' pure path, import-guard NOOP branches) are credited by the integration run's measurement of the installed-backend paths, not by an independent no-extras measurement.
findings.why_real: Verifiable from the two job step commands. It is a genuine (if minor) gap: the coverage gate cannot detect regressions in core-lane-only code paths, because the only lane that computes coverage is the one where backends are present and many fallbacks are skipped. It will not cause CI to go red; it is a measurement-blindspot, hence low severity.
findings.suggested_fix: Run coverage in the core lane too (e.g. `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=xml` on at least the 3.12 core leg) and combine with the integration run, or accept the gap explicitly with a comment. If kept single-lane, document that the floor reflects the all-extras configuration only.

result.reasoning:
The finding's individual code facts are all accurate (verified by reading seam.py:341-369, 258-267, 525-540 and test_reasoning.py:363-381): the positional httpx.Timeout(BULK_TIMEOUT, connect=..., pool=...) does set both read AND write to BULK_TIMEOUT while only reducing connect/pool; the outer bound at line 537 is BULK_TIMEOUT+10; the worker is not cancelled on FuturesTimeout (lines 264-267); and the test asserts timeout.read but never timeout.write. So the literal wording of the comment ("TOTAL stays ~BULK_TIMEOUT") does technically omit the write phase.

However, this is REFUTED as a real production defect for two converging reasons:

1) Out-of-scope by the loopback/on-box design. The bulk tier hard-requires a loopback base URL (require_loopback_url at seam.py:341; BULK_BASE_URL is local) — it talks to a local Ollama on the same box (data-sovereign on-box design). The httpx write-phase timeout governs the gap between write operations while sending the request body; a write-phase stall requires a server that accepts the connection then stalls mid-body-consumption (slow-loris). Against a localhost backend with no intervening network, on a request body of a few KB (prompt + messages), there is no realistic mechanism to stall the write phase to BULK_TIMEOUT. The adversarial slow-loris write vector the finding invokes does not apply to a same-box loopback peer.

2) The finding self-concedes the realism gap. It rates the issue "low" precisely because "LLM request bodies are small, so the write phase rarely stalls," and the existing comment already reasons through the dominant realistic worst case (slow connect THEN slow read). The omitted write phase is the one phase that cannot meaningfully stall given the small-body + loopback constraints. The claimed slot-leak/saturation-erosion failure mode therefore cannot actually be triggered in production; it is a theoretical contradiction of a comment's literal phrasing rather than a reachable defect.

Net: a true-but-immaterial documentation/test-coverage nit against an architecturally-constrained loopback backend, not a genuine production defect — default to REFUTED holds.

Confirmed by reading the code and by execution. is_local_socket_path() (src/assay_engine/_local.py:41-53) only checks the leading characters (startswith('/') and not '//'/'/\\'); it never inspects for a comma. libpq treats a comma in the host value as a multi-host separator, so host='/tmp,evil.com' = [Unix socket /tmp, then TCP evil.com tried in order].

All execution claims reproduced verbatim:
- is_local_socket_path('/tmp,evil.com') -> True
- is_local_socket_path('/var/run/postgresql,attacker.com:5432') -> True
- require_local_uri('host=/tmp,evil.com dbname=x', what='t') -> ACCEPT
- require_local_uri('postgresql:///db?host=/tmp,evil.com', what='t') -> ACCEPT
- require_local_uri('postgresql://localhost/db?host=/tmp,evil.com', what='t') -> ACCEPT
- require_local_uri('host=/var/run/postgresql,attacker.com dbname=assay', what='t') -> ACCEPT

Root cause is exactly as described: the URI-query branch (lines 149-157) and the libpq DSN branch (lines 160-170) accept any value for which is_local_socket_path() returns True, and neither branch splits the value on commas. The multi-host defense (_authority_hosts comma-split, audit #D3) is wired ONLY into the authority branch (lines 141-148), not into these two branches. The socket-path acceptance added for K-SEC-1 thus reopened the multi-host hole for socket-shaped values.

This is a real production defect: it is a deliberate ADR-0003 data-sovereignty guard whose entire purpose is to reject off-box endpoints, and it is on the live operator/env-supplied path — checkpoint.py:163 (get_postgres_connection_string, ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL) and observability/tracking.py:30 (MLflow tracking URI) both route through require_local_uri. When the local socket is unavailable (missing/wrong dir/permission denied), libpq falls through to TCP attacker.com, silently shipping checkpoint/experiment state off-box — the exact failure ADR-0003 forbids. Not out-of-scope, not already-mitigated, not honest-scope. Finding STANDS.

Both falsifiable signals in the finding are confirmed by the actual repo state.

Signal 1 (no enforceable required check): `gh api repos/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/branches/main/protection` returns HTTP 403 "Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public to enable this feature." Branch protection is unavailable on this plan, so no required status check (all-checks, core, integration, or audit) can be configured to gate merges. The all-checks job comment at ci.yml line 223 itself states branch protection "should require ONLY all-checks" — aspirational, not enforced.

Signal 2 (red branches actually merged to main): `gh run list --branch main` shows merge-commit push runs for PRs #161, #162, #166, #167, #168 (and #157, pass-1, pass-2) all with conclusion=failure. Job-level inspection of two of them (runs 27697357334 for #168, 27669521217 for #167) shows genuine failures — core/integration/audit jobs failing and the all-checks fan-in job itself failing — not benign skips/cancellations. CI is configured `on: push: branches:[main]` and `pull_request`, so a failing PR run never prevents the merge.

The all-checks fan-in job (ci.yml lines 227-242) is correctly designed as a single gate, but it is purely decorative without an enforced required status check, which this plan does not permit. This is not refuted by any in-scope exemption: it is not dataset-agnostic-engine/optional-backend/scrubbed-transcript/data-sovereignty scope; it is not already-mitigated (the gate exists but cannot be wired to enforce); and it is not documented honest scope (the design comment frames branch protection as the intended enforcement mechanism, so its absence is a real gap). The run history proves the exact failure mode — CI silently red across multiple passes while merges proceeded.

Every checkable claim in P9-PII-1 verified against live system and committed code/data:

1) Hostname is real and operator/infra-identifying: `scutil --get LocalHostName` and `ComputerName` return `[REDACTED]`; `hostname` returns `[REDACTED]`. Operator memory designates this Mac mini as the named production node.

2) No scrub rule covers it, and it is NOT caught: `_SECRET_PATTERNS` (capture_transcripts.py:38-56) plus the derived `_username_pattern`/`_display_name_patterns`/`_repo_handle_patterns` cover email, home-dir username, project-dir slug, display name, and GitHub handle only. Running `capture_transcripts._scrub('ran on [REDACTED] node')` returns the string UNCHANGED; same for the PROV-O namespace `hcgrc: <https://hc-grc.local/prov#>`.

3) It ships in the repo: 22 raw `[REDACTED]`/`hc-grc.local` occurrences across the 3 cited files, all confirmed git-tracked (git ls-files --error-unmatch succeeds).

4) At least one occurrence is genuine data, not meta-discussion: a user-role message embeds `PREFIX hcgrc: <https://hc-grc.local/prov#>` read from a real hc-grc project file (an internal IP 192.168.1.50 appears nearby), confirming the finding's specific example.

5) Distinct identity class from prior passes (handle/name/username/email/ReDoS).

Refutation paths fail: no README/GOVERNANCE caveat exempts hostnames (grep found none); the only documented scrub caveat concerns reasoning content and path structure. The scrubber exists precisely to mask operator identity in intentionally-committed provenance transcripts (#F-024/#G-007/#J-002/#J-008), so an uncovered identity class is a gap in that mitigation, not an in-scope design choice. This is a real, unmitigated PII/provenance leak. Severity medium is consistent with the prior accepted identity-leak findings.

Verified against the actual code/corpus at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay. The finding's two empirical pillars do not hold up as a production defect.

PILLAR 1 — "email caveat is stale, emails ARE redacted." This is factually true (grep shows 59 occurrences of `[REDACTED]@protonmail` and zero real operator local-parts; the only non-redacted forms are the test fixture `anything@protonmail` and `+@protonmail`; scrubber rule at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:50 redacts emails). But the README caveat (transcripts/README.md:24-30) is, by design, a CONSERVATIVE pre-publication floor: "credential-shaped secrets ARE redacted; transcripts STILL contain paths and MAY contain email + reasoning; review before publishing." Saying email "may" still be present when it is actually scrubbed UNDER-promises on redaction — it pushes the reader to review more, not less. The source docstring (capture_transcripts.py:11-13) explicitly frames this as "Honest scope." An over-cautious caveat is documented honest-scope, not a pre-publication hazard.

PILLAR 2 (the finding's novel claim) — "`[REDACTED]` appears raw and is an unmentioned residual identity vector." This is FALSE. All 12 occurrences live in exactly two files, and every single one is META-DISCUSSION: prior audit-subagent grep commands, their tool-result dumps, and their reasoning text ("the hostname `[REDACTED]` does NOT appear in transcripts", "is it operator PII?", "one more class: the hostname `[REDACTED]`"). There is not one occurrence of the hostname as a genuine operator artifact (no git author `@[REDACTED]`, no real shell prompt). The string exists in the corpus only because earlier skeptics typed it into their own audit reasoning. It is self-referential audit chatter, not an operator-identity leak — a reader gains nothing re-identifying from it beyond what the audit text already labels a non-leak. The sibling `hc-grc.local` matches are synthetic PROV-O RDF namespace URIs, not hostnames.

Net: the README is an intentionally conservative, honest-scope publication caveat; its email wording under-promises (safe direction), and the claimed residual `[REDACTED]` vector is not a real leak but meta-discussion artifact. Not a real production defect.

VERIFIED CODE LOGIC (finding is technically correct on the narrow point): The handler at src/assay_engine/observability/tracing.py:159-163 only handles callable(prior) (chain) and prior == signal.SIG_DFL (reset+re-deliver). I confirmed via the live interpreter that signal.getsignal's docstring documents "None -- if an unknown handler is in effect" (C-installed handler), and that for prior=None all three checks are False: callable(None)=False, None==SIG_DFL=False, None==SIG_IGN=False. So for prior=None the handler flushes, shuts down, and returns, replacing SIGTERM's prior action with flush-and-return — the same swallow-the-signal failure mode K-REL-1 targeted. The fix's own inline comment even mis-states the case space ("Otherwise prior is SIG_DFL/SIG_IGN — both NON-callable"), omitting the documented None case. As a defense-in-depth completeness matter, the gap is genuine.

WHY REFUTED (not a real production defect in this codebase's actual runtime / out-of-scope by design): The finding's realism argument rests entirely on runtime contexts that do not exist here. pyproject.toml declares this as a "Dataset-agnostic engine" with dependencies = [] (dependency-free core); there is no gunicorn, uvicorn, fastapi, flask, or celery in the deps or anywhere in src/. The cited "common" triggers (gunicorn workers, embedded interpreters, native signalfd shims) are not part of this on-box CLI study-runner deployment. _install_flush_on_exit only arms the SIGTERM handler (1) on the main thread (line 146 guard), (2) when the observability extra is installed, and (3) when tracing is enabled — and it runs inside bootstrap_tracing() during normal CLI startup. In a plain CPython CLI process (the actual deployment), the main-thread SIGTERM disposition is SIG_DFL, which the fix handles correctly via reset+re-deliver. For prior to be None, a C extension would have to have installed a native SIGTERM handler on the main thread BEFORE bootstrap_tracing runs; the reasoning backends (anyio/asyncio) use loop.add_signal_handler, which registers a Python callable (caught by the callable(prior) chain branch) and runs under anyio.run rather than before main-thread bootstrap. So the None branch is not reachable in the documented runtime. This is a hardening-completeness nit on an unreachable case for a dataset-agnostic, dependency-free, on-box engine — not a triggered production defect — so it is REFUTED per the default-to-REFUTED / out-of-scope-by-design criteria.

Verified by reading the code and reproducing live. The defect is real, falsifiable, and in-scope.

Code confirms (all four locations real):
- seam.py:94 `BULK_TIMEOUT = max(1.0, float(_env("ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT","120")))` and lines 99,102,103,104,105,109,110 all wrap a bare `int()`/`float()` over `_env()` at module scope. Note: `_env` (seam.py:81-83) only returns a default-substituted string — the int/float conversion around it is still bare and unguarded. The finding's phrasing "over os.environ" is slightly loose but the failure mode is exactly as claimed.
- checkpoint.py:61 `_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS = max(0, int(os.environ.get("ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS","30000")))` — bare int() at import scope.
- checkpoint.py:271-273 (ASSAY_POOL_MIN_SIZE/MAX_SIZE/CONNECT_TIMEOUT) — bare int() at runtime.

Live reproduction (`source .venv/bin/activate`):
- `ASSAY_BULK_TIMEOUT=abc python -c 'import assay_engine.reasoning.seam'` -> `ValueError: could not convert string to float: 'abc'` at seam.py:94, import time.
- `ASSAY_MIGRATION_LOCK_TIMEOUT_MS=xyz python -c 'import assay_engine.persistence.checkpoint'` -> `ValueError: invalid literal for int() with base 10: 'xyz'` at checkpoint.py:61, import time.
Both fire before any handler and name neither the env var nor the expected format.

Fix-regression confirmed: tracing.py:32-43 (`_int_env`, tagged #CV-S-1 / #H-022) and vectorstore.py:20-29 (`_int_env`, tagged #H-022) both wrap int() in a named-error helper that raises `ValueError(f"{name} must be an integer; got {raw!r}")` explicitly to avoid the opaque import-time crash. seam.py and checkpoint.py were left unpatched against the identical class.

Not refutable under any exemption: this is operator-facing config parsing in core production modules (the documented reasoning seam and the persistence/checkpoint backend), not an optional import-guarded backend, dataset-specific logic, scrubbed transcript, or documented honest-scope item. It is a genuine, if low-severity, fix-regression defect — fails loud on misconfiguration but with an unhelpful, unattributed import-time crash.

The finding's mechanical observations are accurate (I reproduced both): pipeline.py:756-758 _log_trail uses json.dumps(..., default=str) while pipeline.py:240-243 persist_trail uses json.dumps() with no default, and ProvenanceTrail.record accepts hashable-but-non-JSON leaves (datetime/bytes), so the persist_trail path raises TypeError while the _log_trail path succeeds on the identical payload. But the finding's core thesis — that persist_trail is the "broken primary persistence path" that should be fixed to match _log_trail's "successful" behavior — is wrong, and the framing inverts which path is correct.

Decisive test: the default=str artifact _log_trail writes does NOT survive the documented round-trip. Reloading it via from_records/verify_records fails with "content was altered ... (hash mismatch)" — because _digest is computed over the original in-memory bytes/datetime payload, while default=str lossily stringifies those leaves (datetime -> '2020-01-01 00:00:00+00:00', b'secret' -> "b'secret'"). So _log_trail produces a GREEN-BUT-CORRUPTED, non-verifiable artifact. persist_trail raising TypeError (failing closed) on the same input is the CORRECT behavior for a durable cryptographic audit trail whose docstring promises re-verifiability via from_records/verify_records — a trail that cannot be exactly serialized must not be silently written in a form that won't verify.

The two paths SHOULD diverge by design: track()/_log_trail is explicitly best-effort observability ("a tracker failure never aborts a run"; failures are caught, recorded, and warned), an optional decorative artifact attached to an optional experiment tracker — never the source of truth. persist_trail is the durable audit path. There is no documented contract that they produce identical bytes. The K-OBS-2 fix's actual purpose (verified in the comment at 748-758) was narrow and legitimate: stop a serialization TypeError from (a) orphaning a NamedTemporaryFile and (b) silently dropping the #G-009 artifact with no operator signal — it now warns. That is a real best-effort-observability hardening, unrelated to the durable-trail contract.

The "sensitive bytes now land stringified" sub-claim is also weak: production internal record() calls (pipeline.py:315/339/etc.) pass only JSON-safe scalars; the only vector for a non-JSON leaf is operator-supplied gate evidence, and even then the artifact is lossy/non-authoritative observability. No real production defect: the divergence is intentional (lossy best-effort artifact vs. fail-closed durable audit trail), and persist_trail's raise is correct, not broken.

The finding's structural facts are accurate: in .github/workflows/ci.yml the `core` job runs bare `pytest -q` (no coverage), and only the `integration` job runs `pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-fail-under=90`. But the finding's central rationale — that the integration run "credits" core-lane-only fallback branches by measuring the installed-backend paths, creating a blindspot — is factually wrong, and that defeats the finding.

Direct evidence from running the suite with coverage in the all-extras venv (numpy 2.4.6 present):
- src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py reports 96% with MISSING lines 155-156 and 163. Lines 155-156 are exactly the pure-Python fallback the finding names: `except ImportError: return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)`. In the backend-present (integration) lane these lines show as UNCOVERED, not "credited." The opposite of the finding's claim.
- The import-guard NOOP branches the finding cites (e.g. graph.py:33, gatenode.py:139, checkpoint.py:240, vectorstore.py:55, tracking.py:58, seam.py:344/425) are explicitly annotated `# pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra`. They are excluded from the coverage denominator, so the integration lane neither credits nor needs to credit them. There is nothing for a no-extras coverage run to "independently" measure that would change the gate.

So the mechanism the finding describes does not exist: backend-present coverage does not falsely credit the fallback branches; those branches are either counted as honest misses (still passing the 90 floor at the real ~96.6%) or pragma-excluded by design. The pure-Python primitive paths that DO matter (e.g. _cosine_matrix_pure) are additionally tested by direct calls in tests/test_baseline.py (lines 224-227, 257-258), so they are covered in the integration lane regardless of numpy presence — again contradicting the blindspot premise.

This is also documented honest-scope: pyproject.toml carries `fail_under = 90` as a defense-in-depth floor, the integration lane is intentionally the coverage-bearing lane because it exercises real backends, and PASS-8.md/PASS-1.md show the campaign deliberately reasoned about per-lane execution fidelity and the (now-fixed) coverage-gate vacuity (K-OPS-3). Running a second --cov pass in the no-extras core lane would only re-measure pure paths already covered or pragma-excluded; it would not catch any regression the current gate misses.

The finding even self-classifies as a non-defect ("will not cause CI to go red; it is a measurement-blindspot"). Combined with the false causal premise and the import-guard branches being intentionally pragma-excluded by design, this is not a real production defect.

Reproduced live for both primitives. With a locked hypothesis whose alpha defaults to None (a documented, first-class state — hypothesis.py:79 "Left None for exploratory/opted-out use"), calling confirm_unit_level(..., alpha=None) and confirm_whole_corpus(..., alpha=None) both raise `TypeError: '<' not supported between instances of 'float' and 'NoneType'` rather than the module's contract error `ValueError: alpha must be in (0, 0.5)`.

Mechanism confirmed by reading code: _resolve_locked_param(None, None, "alpha") returns None (confirm.py:278-285: locked is None -> returns supplied, which is None). That None flows into _validate_alpha(None) (reached via verdict_from_pvalue at line 133 for unit-level, and directly at line 372 for whole-corpus). _validate_alpha (lines 83-88) executes `0.0 < alpha`, i.e. `0.0 < None`, which raises the opaque TypeError before the `< 0.5` clause or the formatted ValueError is ever reached.

Live reproduction (.venv, both paths): unit_level -> TypeError; whole_corpus -> TypeError. Direct _validate_alpha(None) -> TypeError; _resolve_locked_param(None, None, 'alpha') -> None. All as the finding describes.

Not REFUTED on any criterion: (1) Not out-of-scope by design — the confirm.py docstrings explicitly document direct study use of these primitives outside a runner (lines 73-74, 169-170), making them public methodology API; the runners (adjudication.py, discovery.py) contain no alpha handling at all (grep returned nothing), so they pass the argument through and provide no upstream guard. (2) Not dataset-specific, not import-guarded backend, not transcripts/sovereignty/docs-scope. (3) Real defect: the module's stated purpose (issue #4) is to validate confirmatory inputs loudly with typed errors; every other invalid alpha (0.0, 0.5, 1.0) correctly yields the named ValueError, and only the documented-valid None state escapes as an internal numeric-comparison TypeError naming neither the parameter nor the valid range — a genuine violation of the module's own typed-error contract.

The single mitigating fact (it fails loudly rather than producing a silently-wrong result, so only error type/message quality is defective, not correctness) is already acknowledged in the finding and is consistent with its medium severity. It does not negate the defect. Relevant file: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/confirm.py (lines 83-88, 133, 207, 362, 372, 270-285); supporting context /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/methodology/hypothesis.py (line 79, 93-96).
